agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that automatically adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant ambiguity around guardrails, thresholds, and competitive benchmarks.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing product that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to some baseline or capacity — exact definition unspecified"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds unspecified"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied: the standard delivery price before dynamic adjustment is applied (not explicitly stated but logically required)"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to base price as a function of demand level"},{"term":"Competitive","definition":"Pricing positioned relative to market competitors — no benchmark defined"},{"term":"Profitable","definition":"Pricing must cover costs and contribute margin — no margin target defined"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining pricing behavior. A VP could change the pricing strategy (e.g., flat pricing during promotions). The system is not corrupt if this rule is temporarily suspended.","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, the delivery price offered to customers must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy. Management could decide to hold floor prices during low demand instead of discounting. System integrity is not affected.","testable_assertion":"Given demand is below the low-demand threshold, the delivery price offered to customers must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time, not on a batch or scheduled basis","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational preference. The system would not be corrupt if prices updated every 5 minutes vs. every second. The definition of 'real time' is unspecified and negotiable.","testable_assertion":"Given a change in demand level, the price adjustment must be reflected within [undefined SLA] of the demand change being detected"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The pricing system must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for standard price adjustments","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy. A human override or approval step could be added without corrupting the system.","testable_assertion":"Given a demand change occurs, the system must compute and apply a new price without requiring human approval"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A price ceiling guardrail must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum threshold","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without an upper bound, the system could produce arbitrarily high prices that are legally, reputationally, or ethically untenable. This is a data integrity constraint — a price of $999,999 for a local delivery means the system is corrupt. Physical and business reality demands a ceiling.","testable_assertion":"No delivery price produced by DynaPricing may exceed the configured maximum price ceiling"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A price floor guardrail must exist to prevent prices from falling below a minimum threshold","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without a lower bound, the system could produce zero or negative prices, meaning the company pays customers to deliver. This violates basic financial integrity — the system state is corrupt if it quotes a non-positive price.","testable_assertion":"No delivery price produced by DynaPricing may be less than the configured minimum price floor"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The price floor must ensure profitability — the minimum price must cover the cost of fulfillment","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy. Companies regularly price below cost for strategic reasons (market entry, promotions, loss leaders). A VP could authorize below-cost pricing.","testable_assertion":"The configured price floor must be greater than or equal to the per-delivery cost basis (unless explicitly overridden by policy)"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"The pricing function must be monotonically related to demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If higher demand produces a lower price, the system is producing nonsensical output. This is a logical consistency constraint on the pricing function itself — violating it means the algorithm is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, price(D1) >= price(D2)"},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"Price adjustments must be applied consistently to all customers seeing the same demand conditions","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a fairness/business policy. The company might later introduce customer tiers, loyalty discounts, or A/B pricing. System is not corrupt if different customers see different prices.","testable_assertion":"Given two customers requesting delivery in the same zone at the same time with the same service level, the quoted dynamic price must be identical (absent customer-tier differentiation)"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand in a delivery zone exceeds the high-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price quote","then":"The quoted price is higher than the base price for that zone and service level"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand in a delivery zone is below the low-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price quote","then":"The quoted price is lower than the base price for that zone and service level"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand reaches an extreme spike","when":"The pricing engine computes an adjusted price","then":"The price is capped at the configured ceiling and never exceeds it"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand drops to near zero","when":"The pricing engine computes an adjusted price","then":"The price never falls below the configured floor"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A demand change is detected","when":"The system processes the demand signal","then":"A new price is computed and available to quoting systems within the real-time SLA (SLA TBD)"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The pricing engine is operating normally","when":"Any price adjustment occurs","then":"No human approval or manual step is required"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The configured price floor is set below the cost of fulfillment","when":"The system starts or configuration is updated","then":"A warning is emitted and the configuration is flagged for review"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand signal is unavailable or stale (e.g., data feed outage)","expected_behavior":"System must have a fallback — likely revert to base price or last known good price. Must NOT produce $0 or null prices. This is unspecified in the CEO's description."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly around a threshold boundary","expected_behavior":"Price must not flicker between high and low states. Some hysteresis or smoothing is implied but unspecified."},{"scenario":"Two adjacent delivery zones have wildly different demand levels","expected_behavior":"Customers near zone boundaries could game the system by shifting pickup/dropoff. Zone arbitrage policy is unspecified."},{"scenario":"Sudden demand spike from external event (e.g., concert, weather emergency)","expected_behavior":"Guardrail ceiling must hold. Reputational risk of surge pricing during emergencies — CEO says 'guardrails' but does not address emergency/disaster scenarios specifically."},{"scenario":"Price floor is set higher than price ceiling in configuration","expected_behavior":"System must reject this as invalid configuration. This is a configuration integrity invariant."},{"scenario":"Demand metric is artificially inflated (e.g., bot requests, DDoS on quoting)","expected_behavior":"Demand signal should measure genuine delivery intent, not raw request volume. Fraud/gaming vector is unspecified."},{"scenario":"Launch day — no historical demand data exists","expected_behavior":"Cold-start pricing strategy is unspecified. System needs a default or seed demand model."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue and margin. Correctly implemented, it captures surplus during high demand and stimulates volume during low demand. Incorrectly implemented, it loses money (floor too low) or loses customers (ceiling too high, no competitive check).","operational":"Full automation reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational risk — a bug in the pricing algorithm affects every transaction instantly. Monitoring and circuit-breaker capabilities are essential but unspecified.","user":"Customers will perceive price variability. Transparency of pricing rationale is unspecified. Risk of customer backlash if prices feel unfair or unpredictable (the 'Uber surge pricing' problem).","risk":"HIGH — Regulatory risk in some jurisdictions (price gouging laws during emergencies), reputational risk from perceived unfairness, financial risk from algorithm errors affecting all transactions simultaneously. No mention of audit trail, price justification logging, or regulatory compliance.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — will see variable prices. May feel unfairly charged during high demand. May benefit from lower prices during low demand. Transparency and trust are at stake."},{"group":"Delivery Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Indirectly affected — if dynamic pricing drives volume shifts, courier workload and earnings may fluctuate. Courier compensation model interaction is unspecified."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Must understand and forecast revenue under variable pricing. Need reporting and analytics on pricing distribution."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Demand signals that feed pricing also affect dispatch. Tight coupling risk between pricing and operations systems."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review pricing guardrails against price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and fair pricing requirements. NOT mentioned by CEO."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Need pricing explanation tools and policies."},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"Competitive response risk — if competitors monitor and undercut dynamic prices, the system may need competitive intelligence inputs."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a 3-sentence vision statement with no specification rigor. Almost every concrete parameter is undefined: demand thresholds, price boundaries, real-time SLA, demand measurement method, guardrail values, competitive benchmarks, and cost basis. The word 'probably' before guardrails introduces uncertainty about whether guardrails are even confirmed requirements. Multiple critical domains (legal, courier impact, zone definition) are entirely unaddressed. This is a napkin sketch, not a spec.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System is automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"],"low_confidence_areas":["What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined","What the guardrail values are — no ceiling or floor specified","What 'real time' means — no SLA defined","How demand is measured — no metric defined","What 'competitive' means — no benchmark or competitor data source defined","What 'profitable' means — no margin target or cost basis defined","Whether guardrails are hard requirements or aspirational ('probably')","Regulatory and legal compliance requirements","How pricing interacts with courier compensation","Zone/geography model for demand measurement"],"assumptions":["A base price exists that dynamic adjustments are applied to","Demand is measurable in real time via some existing or new signal","Guardrails will be implemented despite the CEO's 'probably' hedge","Price floor will be positive (company will not pay customers to deliver)","The pricing function is monotonic with respect to demand","Some form of zone or geographic segmentation exists for demand measurement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO said guardrails will 'probably' exist. Is this a confirmed requirement or aspirational? Without guardrails, the system has no safety net. This is the single most important architectural decision and it is hedged.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Finance"],"options":["Treat guardrails as mandatory (interpret 'probably' as 'definitely')","Treat guardrails as optional and build without them initially","Build guardrails but make them configurable/disableable"],"recommendation":"Treat guardrails as mandatory. The legal, financial, and reputational risk of unbounded pricing is unacceptable. Seek explicit CEO confirmation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No definition of demand metric. Is demand measured by: order volume per hour? Orders per courier? Queue depth? Ratio of orders to available couriers? The entire pricing model depends on this definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Orders per time window per zone","Order-to-courier ratio per zone","Queue depth / wait time as demand proxy"],"recommendation":"Order-to-available-courier ratio is the most operationally meaningful demand signal in logistics. Seek product confirmation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No guardrail values specified. What is the maximum price multiplier? What is the minimum price? Without these, acceptance criteria cannot be fully verified.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Finance","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["Define as absolute values (e.g., $5 min, $50 max)","Define as multipliers of base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3x)","Define as percentage of competitor benchmark"],"recommendation":"Multiplier-based guardrails (e.g., 0.7x to 2.5x base price) are industry standard for logistics surge pricing. Seek finance team input on acceptable range."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have price gouging laws that cap price increases during emergencies. Dynamic pricing systems in logistics must account for this.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Customers"],"options":["Build emergency override that freezes prices","Build jurisdiction-aware guardrails","Defer regulatory compliance to phase 2"],"recommendation":"Legal review must happen before launch. Build emergency price-freeze capability as a day-one requirement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive' is stated as a goal but no competitive benchmark or data source is defined. Is the system supposed to monitor competitor prices? Or is 'competitive' just a general aspiration?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product"],"options":["Integrate competitor price monitoring","Define 'competitive' as staying within a fixed range of base price","Treat as aspirational, not systematic"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team. If competitive means reactive to market, this is a fundamentally different system than demand-only pricing."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant ambiguity around guardrails, thresholds, and competitive benchmarks.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing product that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"te
